podcasts.name: The MAD Podcast with Matt Turck
podcasts.title: The Biggest Chip Ever Built — Why OpenAI Runs On It | Cerebras CEO Andrew Feldman
podcasts.transcript:
Speaker 1 | 00:00 - 00:21
This is the largest chip built in the history of the computer industry. It's 58 times larger than a GPU. And for AI, bigger chips process information more quickly, and therefore, you get answers in less time. For AI world, big chips are undoubtedly the best way to go. There's no mode in inference.

Speaker 1 | 00:21 - 00:36
It takes you eight keystrokes to move from a GPU to us in the cloud. We solved a problem that nobody in the history of computing solved, and we delivered it in 2020, and nobody cared. Nobody cared. Nobody bought it in. Nobody cared.

Speaker 1 | 00:36 - 00:40
Everybody said we were crazy. It would never work. So then we built the next one.

Speaker 2 | 00:40 - 01:02
Hi. I'm Matt Turk. Welcome to the MAD podcast. My guest today is Andrew Feldman, cofounder and CEO of Cerubrus, the company that built the largest chip in the history of computing and just pulled off the biggest semiconductor IPO of all time. Andrew has been everywhere talking about the headlines, the $20,000,000,000 plus OpenAI deal, the IPO, but this conversation is a bit different.

Speaker 2 | 01:02 - 01:28
We started from what is a wafer and built up step by step, Why GPUs struggle with fast inference, the three shortages nobody talks about, the decade in the desert when nobody wanted this chip, and why Andrew believes that CUDA is no longer a moat for NVIDIA. If you want to actually understand the current chip landscape and how AI inference works at the silicon level, this episode is for you. Please enjoy this fantastic conversation with Andrew Feldman.

Speaker 3 | 01:31 - 01:41
I thought a a fun place to start would be to talk about speed. So has speed become the dominant conversation for AI today?

Speaker 1 | 01:41 - 01:59
Yeah. What happened, I think, was for a long time, AI was sort of a novelty. It was like a parlor trick. It was cool, but not useful. And what happened somewhere around the middle of twenty twenty five was the AI got smart enough such that people began to use it.

Speaker 1 | 02:00 - 02:15
And we remember, we we we make AI with training, but we use it with inference. And suddenly people wanted to use it. And the minute you wanna use it, the minute it's productive, right, speed matters. Right? Fast tokens are more productive.

Speaker 1 | 02:15 - 02:32
And so the conversation moved from everything else to how do we make our inference faster? How do we deliver tokens more quickly? Because those are more productive tokens. We get more done in less time, and, therefore, it's more valuable.

Speaker 3 | 02:32 - 02:39
Great. And what what does speed mean? Is that is that a a question of token speed? Is that completion of the task? What what's the right metric?

Speaker 3 | 02:39 - 02:40
Sure.

Speaker 1 | 02:40 - 03:06
The right metric is tokens per second per user. That that's how fast you get the first token all the way through the last token in in your in your response. And it's true for queries from from chat, but it's also true for for agentic floats. Right? If they're sort of multicycle turns, waiting is amplified.

Speaker 1 | 03:06 - 03:16
And so what you want is blisteringly fast responses so that the the AI feels like it's in real time. You can engage with this. K? So

Speaker 3 | 03:16 - 03:20
it's the Brombeil moment for Post for AI. I think

Speaker 1 | 03:20 - 03:43
that's I think that's right. And I I think that's a very good analogy. I I think if you think of something like like Netflix, right, when the Internet was slow, right, Netflix delivered DVDs and envelopes. You would get a a DVD at an envelope. And when the Internet became fast, they didn't get more efficient at delivering DMUDs and envelopes.

Speaker 1 | 03:43 - 03:54
They became a movie studio. Mhmm. Right? The speed enabled them to become something completely different. And that's what speed does in general and in particular for for AI.

Speaker 1 | 03:54 - 04:05
It it opens up a whole new domain. It allows you to use the AI differently. You will stay longer. You you will come more often, and you work on harder problems.

Speaker 3 | 04:05 - 04:11
Yep. So it's literally a question of of the UX. Right? That's just nobody wants to wait a few seconds.

Speaker 1 | 04:11 - 04:18
Yeah. That's great. I mean, how big is the market for slow search? How big is the market for dial up? It's zero.

Speaker 1 | 04:18 - 04:26
How big is how long will you wait for a website to resolve? Will you wait eight seconds? Nobody waits. See? And so it's the exact same with AI.

Speaker 3 | 04:26 - 04:30
Yep. So no more people waiting with their laptops open while the agent

Speaker 1 | 04:30 - 04:36
That's right. Well, it's running and running and running. I I I think that is not what what people want.

Speaker 3 | 04:36 - 04:57
Okay. Great. Wonderful. So I'd love to talk about the landscape of the chip industry right now to help people visualize an artwork where where you guys are. So there used to be basically this concept of, like, one chip to do it all.

And, obviously, this is evolving dramatically. This you guys, this this Grok, this there are GPUs. There are then people have ever heard of Tranium. People have run those GPUs. So help us compare and contrast who does what for what.

Speaker 1 | 05:16 - 05:26
Well, I I think there used to be one chip to do it all. Was called the CPU. Yeah. Right? And there emerged a coprocessor to do discrete graphics.

Speaker 1 | 05:27 - 05:54
And as the AI workload became interesting, we focused chips more and more on that particular workload. And today, several companies make traditional GPUs. So NVIDIA and AMD make very standard GPUs. There are a group of companies who the hyperscalers make make some of their own parts. So the TPU.

So TPU is Google. TPU is

Google, and training them is is AWS. Yep. And then there were group like Strabrous, and I we were among the pioneers to to to build a part from scratch optimized for AI and nothing else. And that we weren't inside of a hyperscaler for we weren't optimized for a hyperscalers problem or for one lab's problem. We we were building a chip for a collection of AI problems.

And all our thinking was around how to accelerate AI. And that's sort of the landscape to this day.

Yeah. This even more specialized ones. Right? Like etched Sure. Developed specific waveformers.

I get Then those are called ASICs as well. Could you can you maybe define what that term means?

An ASIC is an application specific integrated circuit, and and it's it's a word that that now has a a wide range of of meaning. It it means that you have made a series of choices away from general towards a narrower class of problem solving and that you've made some decisions in your architecture that make it much better at some things and much, much worse than others. Right? And and that's choices that are made across the spectrum. So the TPU has made some choices like that.

It can't do graphics. It's very good at matrix multiply. It's not very good at a collection of other things that we do in in mathematics. Same for the GPU. We we've all made different choices.

I was right now in production. There are in Meridian AMD, there are a collection, there's a TPU for Google, Traniums. Just coming up is a Maya part is a part from Microsoft, Cerberus. I mean, it was one other Grok that got acquired by by NVIDIA.

Yeah. I I think one of those things with NVIDIA's sort of most durable modes was the perception that the GPU could do everything, and it was all you needed for AI. So that that was a good day. That was a good day. And

the recent announcements of Jalapeno between OpenAI, which is a very large customer of yours, and Broadcom, where does that fit in that picture? Is that one of those highly specialized ASICs types? Right. Grok? Because it's for inference.

This is probably the largest deals in Silicon Valley history, north of $20,000,000,000. I I think they have a a yawning need for silicon. Others have had to go out and strike really bad deals to get capacity. Where OpenAI saw this coming, they struck big deals for memory, for compute, with us, with others. It is growing so unbelievably fast. The whole industry is chasing demand. Right? We usually well, often, it's the other way. Often, people are building it, hoping it will come.

And in our case, all of us are are are chasing what people already wanna do, let alone what they might do in the future.

I I put a opinion that I may and I may, because of this, such an interesting topic. To to finish on GPAs versus in France versus a six, Is that the permanent situation from your perspective that we're going to be in this forever multisilicon kind of environment?

Yeah. Multisilicon environment's a healthy ecosystem. Right? I I don't think anybody would say that the x 86 environment was a healthy place. There were for twenty years, there were two players.

And if you notice, when a new workload came around, cell phone workload, that was very similar but required low power and battery and both lost. Right? Intel got zero share. AMD got zero share. And a company nobody previously heard of became the largest seller of compute in the world, and that's R.

No. That that divide is real.

No. I I think they are an industrial adversary that I think come they have made really interesting investments. They've made investments in power in their grid, which is a real weakness in The US. If you're in France, you you have nuclear, which sort of turns out to be pretty cheap,

you know,

power and pretty clean. But in China, they made huge investments in their grid. They are behind in chips. But their approach was at the next level as open source models where they're producing some extraordinary models. Not as good as as as GPT or Anthropic or Google's Gemini, but very good.

Right? And I I think they don't have the the same chip strikes that The US does, but they've got leadership in other domains, in particularly in power, which is what we need for data centers.

And so you wanna do as much as you can, as as close to the data as you can. But the truth is is in most situations, for real compute, you have to go up to the cloud to the data center.

And that's exactly the way it's gonna be with AI. We're gonna do a lot of work on the cell phones, on a laptop. But for the big work, you're gonna go to to the data center. That's where our focus is. Our focus is is data center compute free.

Great. So you mentioned the the how powerful the demand was and given the, you know, right of production. So just to ask the inevitable question around market equilibrium bubble or not. there are three major bottlenecks right now. The first is all GPUs and all ASICs except us use a type of memory called DRAM and a particular flavor of that memory called HBMs. And that memory is made by three companies in the world. The Kydex, Samsung,

and Micronauts.

And this is what in Zadia and AMD and others use as a motherboard, so they put their chips and the memory chips on it, and it's more efficient than a traditional motherboard. So this is the most aggressive factory. So with the the most advanced technology, our chips are at five nanometers, so we don't use the three nanometers technology. And so And and

then that's so fascinating. So, to ask the layman's question, so this this this what what does that mean, three millimeter factory? That's a factory that's solely dedicated to the

that's literally different tooling at the factory? Tooling at the And

Including in a system like yours? Including in a system like ours. It's interesting. So so what what does it mean? So you'll get you have your your chip and CPUs on the side, and and those will be a system?

It means that AI when we do AI in AgenTic, they the AI processor is like the brains, and the CPUs are like the body. They're taking action. And

There's this DRAM of HBM, and they can store a lot, but they're. There's another type of memory called that is unbelievably fast, but per unit area can't store very much. And that for graphics, everybody had always used DRAM. They used HPGR. And that the AI workload was fundamentally different.

In graphics, you move data to the GPU, and then you work on it for a long time. And then you send the result. So the time spent in total of movement plus work is dominated by work. In inference in AI, it's the exact opposite. You move a huge amount of data, all the weights from memory to compute, and you do one calculation to generate the next word.

And then you have to do it again. Yep. So all the time is dominated by the movement of data. So that's why GPUs have so much trouble being fast. So we observed that if we chose a strategy using SRAM, we could be faster.
